title: Reverse Linked List
description:
A singly linked list is represented as a plain list `values`, in head-to-tail order. Return a new list of values representing that same linked list reversed (tail-to-head order).

**Example**

```
values = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]  ->  [5, 4, 3, 2, 1]
```
starter_code:
def reverse_linked_list(values):
    # values: list representing a linked list head-to-tail.
    # Return the values in reversed order.
    pass
hints: The real linked-list technique is an iterative pointer flip: build actual nodes, then walk with prev/curr/next pointers, relinking curr.next = prev as you go.
After the walk, prev points at the new head; read the values off from there to produce the output list.

solutions.name: Build nodes, iterative pointer reversal
solutions.explanation: Build a real singly linked list out of the input values, then reverse it the standard way: keep prev (starts None) and curr (starts at head) pointers, and on each step save curr.next, relink curr.next to point at prev, then advance prev and curr forward by one. When curr runs out, prev is the new head. Finally walk from the new head to read the values back out into a list.
solutions.code:
class _Node:
    __slots__ = ("val", "next")
    def __init__(self, val):
        self.val = val
        self.next = None


def reverse_linked_list(values):
    head = None
    tail = None
    for v in values:
        node = _Node(v)
        if head is None:
            head = tail = node
        else:
            tail.next = node
            tail = node

    prev = None
    curr = head
    while curr is not None:
        nxt = curr.next
        curr.next = prev
        prev = curr
        curr = nxt

    out = []
    node = prev
    while node is not None:
        out.append(node.val)
        node = node.next
    return out
